professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: We've been discussing how many museums acquired their collections during periods of colonial expansion. Recently, several major institutions have returned artifacts to their countries of origin, while others argue these objects reach far wider audiences when kept in large international museums. Should museums be required to return cultural artifacts to the communities they came from? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe artifacts should return to their communities of origin. These objects carry ancestral and religious significance that a foreign display case cannot honor, and their removal often erased local memory. Returning them acknowledges historical wrongs and lets descendants reconnect with their own heritage.

students.name: Paul
students.text: Keeping certain artifacts in international museums serves a genuine purpose. Millions of visitors encounter civilizations they would otherwise never study, and well-funded institutions can preserve fragile pieces that vulnerable regions may lack the resources to protect. Access and conservation shouldn't be dismissed so quickly.

professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: More and more brands are shifting their advertising budgets away from television toward individual social media influencers. Supporters say influencers build authentic trust with niche audiences, while critics counter that paid endorsements blur the line between honest opinion and advertisement. Do you think companies should prioritize influencer partnerships over traditional advertising? Why?

students.name: Andrew
students.text: I think influencer partnerships deliver better value for most brands today. Consumers increasingly ignore conventional commercials, but they follow creators whose recommendations feel personal and relevant. A single trusted voice reaching an engaged community often generates more genuine interest than an expensive, broadly targeted campaign.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: Traditional advertising still offers advantages that influencers cannot match. Established channels reach enormous, diverse audiences at once and give companies full control over their message and image. When an influencer faces controversy, the associated brand can suffer serious reputational damage almost overnight.

professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: We've been discussing how communities memorialize their past through public monuments. Many cities have recently debated removing statues of controversial historical figures: some argue these monuments honor values we now reject, while others believe removing them erases a history we ought to confront. Should such monuments be taken down, or preserved with added context? Why?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe monuments honoring figures tied to oppression should be removed from places of public honor. A statue in a central square celebrates its subject, and forcing marginalized residents to pass it daily is deeply unjust. Museums, not plazas, are the proper setting for such history.

students.name: Andrew
students.text: In my opinion, removing monuments risks sanitizing an uncomfortable past we still need to examine. Adding explanatory plaques that acknowledge a figure's crimes teaches far more than an empty pedestal. Confronting history in public keeps difficult conversations visible rather than quietly forgotten.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: This week, we have been exploring how computing now shapes nearly every profession. Some educators argue that every university student, regardless of major, should complete at least one programming course to stay competitive; others believe such requirements crowd out subjects more central to a student's own field. Should programming be mandatory for all students? Why or why not?

students.name: Paul
students.text: I think a basic programming requirement benefits students across every discipline. Understanding how software works helps historians analyze large datasets and lets artists automate tedious tasks. Even those who never write code again gain a clearer sense of the systems that increasingly govern their careers.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: In my opinion, forcing programming on every student wastes limited academic time. A literature or nursing major already faces demanding coursework central to their profession, and a rushed coding class rarely produces lasting skill. Students who are genuinely interested can elect these courses without a universal mandate.
